summary: Nevada has had a complex history with gambling regulation. From legal gambling starting in 1869 until an anti-gambling law took effect in 1910, the state largely left regulation to local governments. In 1931, Nevada legalized gambling statewide to boost revenues and attract tourists, especially those coming for quick divorces. Over time, the state moved from minimal regulation to more oversight, particularly in 1945 when the Nevada Tax Commission began licensing and taxing gaming. Nevada’s evolving stance on gambling reflects its economic and social shifts throughout the early to mid-20th century.

Governor Emmet Boyle was a Progressive leader in Nevada around 1919 who believed in expanded government regulation and moral reform. He saw the anti-gambling law of 1909 as a failure and pushed for a stricter, enforceable prohibition on all forms of gambling. Despite his efforts, his proposals did not succeed at that time. Boyle’s tenure highlighted early attempts to control vice through government intervention in Nevada.

Reno, Nevada, was a key city in the state’s early gambling and divorce scene, especially in the 1920s and 1930s. Investors like George Wingfield were involved in its casinos, which attracted visitors seeking quick divorces after the residency requirement was cut to three months in 1927 and then six weeks in 1931. Reno also had a reputation for tolerating vice, with its sheriff famously welcoming figures like Al Capone as long as they behaved. The city’s gambling industry grew alongside these social changes, making it a focal point of Nevada’s evolving gaming landscape.

Las Vegas in the 1920s was a small but growing town where tourists arriving via the Union Pacific Railroad could easily access Block 16, known for gambling, prostitution, and illegal alcohol. This area was a hub of vice before gambling was legalized statewide in 1931. The city’s proximity to the railroad depot made it a natural spot for those seeking entertainment and escape during Prohibition. Las Vegas’s early reputation set the stage for its later development as a major gaming center.

The Union Pacific Railroad played a significant role in Nevada’s early gaming and vice scene by bringing tourists and visitors to cities like Las Vegas. People disembarking from the Union Pacific depot in downtown Las Vegas had a short walk to Block 16, an area known for gambling, prostitution, and illegal alcohol during the 1920s. The railroad thus helped facilitate the flow of visitors seeking entertainment and vice in Nevada before gambling was legalized. Its presence was integral to the economic and social dynamics of the region at that time.

Block 16 was a notorious area in downtown Las Vegas during the 1920s known for gambling, prostitution, and illegal alcohol. Visitors arriving by the Union Pacific Railroad could easily reach this district, which catered to those seeking vice during Prohibition. It represented the kind of underground activity that existed openly before gambling was legalized in 1931. Block 16’s reputation was part of the broader context of Nevada’s tolerance for vice in that era.

Construction on Hoover Dam began around the same time as the pivotal 1931 legislative session in Nevada. This period marked a turning point when the state legalized gambling and shortened divorce residency requirements. The dam’s construction brought workers and economic activity to the region, which likely influenced the legislature’s decisions to boost revenues through legalized gambling. Hoover Dam thus serves as a historical marker for Nevada’s shift toward embracing gaming as an economic driver.

Governor Fred Balzar signed two important laws on March 19, 1931, that legalized gambling in Nevada and reduced the residency requirement for divorce to six weeks. His approval of these laws marked a major shift in state policy aimed at increasing revenues and attracting tourists. Balzar’s role was crucial in setting the stage for Nevada’s future as a gaming haven. His governorship coincided with the start of Hoover Dam construction, a time of significant change for the state.

Phil Tobin was a Humboldt County rancher who introduced the gambling bill in the 1931 Nevada legislature. He argued that legalizing gambling would increase state revenues and formalize activities that were already happening unofficially. Tobin’s efforts helped pass the law that legalized gambling immediately, shaping Nevada’s economic future. His role was pivotal in transitioning the state toward regulated gaming.

George Wingfield was Nevada’s political and economic boss during the 1920s and beyond, heavily involved in Reno casinos and banking. He supported the 1931 legalization of gambling, which aligned with his libertarian views and benefited his business interests. Wingfield’s influence helped shape Nevada’s approach to gaming and economic development. He was a key figure in attracting tourists and investors to the state through both gambling and divorce laws.

Al Capone was a notorious gangster who had a presence in Nevada’s gambling scene, particularly in Reno. The local sheriff famously said that Capone was welcome as long as he behaved himself, though Capone rarely did. His friend Frank Detra operated the Pair-O-Dice casino outside Las Vegas, which allegedly received funding linked to Capone. Capone’s involvement highlighted early concerns about organized crime infiltrating Nevada’s gaming industry.

Frank Detra was a friend of Al Capone who opened the Pair-O-Dice casino on Highway 91 outside Las Vegas in 1930. Detra reportedly invested money to support legal gambling in Nevada, some of which may have come from Capone himself. His activities exemplify the early connections between organized crime and Nevada’s gaming industry. Detra’s role is a reminder of the complicated and sometimes shady origins of legalized gambling in the state.

The Pair-O-Dice was a casino opened in 1930 on Highway 91 outside Las Vegas by Frank Detra, a friend of Al Capone. It was part of the early gambling scene before legalization and allegedly had financial ties to Capone. The casino represents the kind of illegal or semi-legal operations that existed prior to the 1931 legalization of gambling in Nevada. Pair-O-Dice’s history illustrates the blurred lines between vice and business in that era.

The Chicago Tribune reacted strongly to Nevada’s 1931 legalization of gambling, publishing a headline that demanded the cancellation of Nevada’s statehood. This national media response contrasted sharply with the more subdued local reaction, which saw legalized gambling as merely bringing secret activities into the open. The Tribune’s stance reflected widespread concern and moral opposition outside Nevada to the state’s new gaming laws. Their coverage highlights the controversy gambling legalization sparked nationwide.

In 1945, the Nevada Tax Commission was given the authority to investigate and decide on all gaming license applicants as the state sought more control over the expanding gambling industry. This move came as organized crime interests became more prominent and the state looked to increase revenue through a one percent tax on gross gaming revenues. Though Governor E.P. Carville was unhappy with the legislation, he allowed it to become law due to its broad support. The Commission’s role marked a significant step toward state-level regulation of gaming.

Governor E.P. Carville was in office in 1945 when the Nevada legislature passed bills to give the Nevada Tax Commission authority over gaming licenses and to tax gaming revenues. Although Carville was unhappy with these bills, he recognized their broad support and allowed them to become law. His governorship marked a turning point where the state began to exert more control and oversight over the gambling industry. Carville’s tenure reflects the growing complexity of managing Nevada’s gaming economy.
